one_line_takeaway: PoiCGAN uses a modified CGAN whose discriminator is trained on deliberately mismatched (source-image, target-label) pairs to produce a single generator that performs feature perturbation and automatic label flipping jointly, yielding targeted FL poisoning that the paper's own experiments show is both more effective (83.97% average ASR, <8.9% ACC drop) and more stealthy (highest MIS; retains 56.5-75% ASR against Krum/RLR/FLAME) than the TDP, TMP, and ADA baselines.
key_finding: Averaged over the three datasets, PoiCGAN achieves ASR 83.97%, beating the three baselines by 15.57 to 30.8 percentage points, while incurring less than an 8.87% (paper states 'less than 8.9%' in the results text) drop in main-task ACC versus the no-attack case (average ACC 85.13% under attack) -- baselines average over 20.1% ACC degradation. Stealthiness (Table 5, InsPLAD, MIS metric): PoiCGAN MIS=375.22 versus TDP=67.49, TMP=58.17, ADA=66.27 -- i.e., PoiCGAN's poisoned-vs-benign model clusters are far closer together in PCA-reduced parameter space than any baseline's. Under defenses (Table 6): against FLAME on InsPLAD, PoiCGAN ASR=56.5% (ACC 86.2%) vs. baselines TDP=8.7%, TMP=13.0%, ADA=8.7%; against RLR on NEU-CLS, PoiCGAN ASR=70.0% (ACC 77.3%) vs. TDP=25.0%, TMP=40.0%, ADA=55.0%; against Krum on Kylberg, PoiCGAN ASR=75.0% (ACC 87.8%) vs. TDP=15.0%, TMP=10.0%, ADA=25.0%. Averaged across the three defense settings, baseline ASRs collapse to 16.23%, 21%, and 29.57% while PoiCGAN retains 67.17% average ASR. Hyperparameter sensitivity: increasing PMR raises ASR but slightly lowers ACC; ASR rises then falls as PSG training rounds T grows (too few rounds -> low-fidelity poison images, weak signal; too many rounds -> poison images converge to true source images, effectively degenerating into a plain label-flip attack that conflicts with the main task) -- T=200 is reported as near-optimal (~80% ASR, ~90% ACC on the tested dataset); increasing scaling factor gamma raises ASR but hurts ACC, with gamma=1 (no scaling) giving the best overall balance in their experiment.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is PoiCGAN, a targeted data-poisoning attack for federated learning (FL)-based industrial image classification, built around a novel core module called the Poison Sample Generator (PSG). PSG is a modified Conditional GAN (CGAN) in which the discriminator is trained on three input types instead of the standard two: (a) real source-class images paired with the (wrong) target label -> labeled 'real', (b) real non-source-class images paired with their correct labels -> labeled 'real', and (c) generator-produced fake images paired with the target label -> labeled 'fake'. Feeding mismatched (source-image, target-label) pairs as positive examples corrupts the discriminator's decision boundary so that, once trained, the generator Gpoi(z|t) synthesizes images that visually resemble the source class but carry the target label as their conditioning input -- i.e., feature perturbation and label flipping happen jointly and automatically from a single generator, using only a small number of clean local samples from the malicious client. This is used within a one-to-one targeted attack (one fixed source class -> one fixed target class) inside standard FedAvg-based FL, with an optional model-replacement-style scaling of malicious updates (scaling factor gamma) borrowed from Bagdasaryan et al.'s FL backdoor work. The paper also proposes a new stealthiness metric, the Model Indistinguishability Score (MIS), defined as the inverse Euclidean distance (in a PCA-reduced 2D parameter space) between the centroid of benign-client models and the centroid of malicious-client models -- higher MIS means the poisoned models are harder to separate from benign ones.
threat_model: FL system for industrial image classification (defect detection / surface analysis), 20 total clients, 10 sampled per round, FedAvg aggregation, 200 communication rounds, 2 local epochs/round. The attacker compromises a fraction k/N of clients (Poisoned Model Rate, PMR; base setting PMR=40%) and has white-box access to those clients' local training data, optimization procedure, and model parameters, per Kerckhoffs's-principle-style adversary modeling consistent with Tolpegin et al. The attacker also knows the server's aggregation rule and any deployed defense, but cannot influence server-side computation or honest clients' training (matches Section 4.1.3). Attacker goals are dual: (1) effectiveness -- force the global model to misclassify all source-class inputs as the target class while (2) stealth -- leave predictions on all other inputs unchanged, keep the poisoned client models visually/statistically close to benign models in parameter space (measured via MIS), and keep poisoned image samples visually close to the originals. Poisoning is applied continuously: malicious clients train exclusively on poisoned data in poisoned rounds, sustained from round 50 onward at fixed PMR and Poisoned Data Ratio.
limitations: The paper's own scope restrictions, stated explicitly: (1) evaluated only on image classification tasks (defect detection/surface analysis); future work section explicitly defers poisoning of text/audio data and of vertical FL / federated transfer learning settings to later work. (2) The attack is restricted to a one-to-one targeted setting (single fixed source class, single fixed target class) by design, to limit collateral main-task degradation -- this is a deliberate design constraint, not evaluated against multi-source/multi-target scenarios. (3) Hyperparameter sensitivity analyses (Sections 5.4.1-5.4.3) each vary one factor while holding the others fixed, and each uses only a single dataset (InsPLAD for PMR, Kylberg for training rounds T, NEU-CLS for scaling factor) rather than all three datasets, so generality of the specific 'optimal' values (PMR=40%, T=200, gamma=1) across datasets is not directly demonstrated. (4) Defense-robustness evaluation (Table 6) pairs each defense with only one dataset (Krum-Kylberg, RLR-NEU-CLS, FLAME-InsPLAD) rather than a full cross-product of defenses x datasets. (5) The attacker's knowledge assumption (aware of the aggregation rule and deployed defenses, full white-box access to compromised clients' data/parameters) is a relatively strong/favorable-to-attacker assumption, following prior FL poisoning literature (Tolpegin et al.) rather than a weaker gray/black-box threat model.

datasets_used: NEU-CLS (steel surface defect dataset, 6 classes: RS, Pa, Cr, PS, In, Sc; 128x128 grayscale; 1260 train / 540 test, 7:3 split)
InsPLAD-fault (power line defect classification, 2023 release; 4 labels covering rust/corrosion/missing-cap/bird-nest across multiple asset types; 128x128 RGB; 2250 train / 146 test)
Kylberg texture dataset (University of Gothenburg; 28 total material texture classes, subset of 6 used per Table 1; 128x128 grayscale; 3920 train / 560 test)
quantitative_results: Average ASR across 3 datasets: PoiCGAN 83.97%, exceeding baselines by 15.57-30.8 percentage points
Average main-task ACC under attack: PoiCGAN 85.13% (drop <8.9% vs no-attack clean ACC); baselines average >20.1% ACC drop
MIS stealthiness on InsPLAD (Table 5): PoiCGAN 375.224762, TDP 67.494881, TMP 58.172310, ADA 66.266571 (higher = more stealthy)
ASR under FLAME defense on InsPLAD: PoiCGAN 56.5% vs TDP 8.7%, TMP 13.0%, ADA 8.7% (ACC 86.2/88.6/87.8/86.2%)
ASR under RLR defense on NEU-CLS: PoiCGAN 70.0% vs TDP 25.0%, TMP 40.0%, ADA 55.0% (ACC 77.3/88.0/82.0/85.0%)
ASR under Krum defense on Kylberg: PoiCGAN 75.0% vs TDP 15.0%, TMP 10.0%, ADA 25.0% (ACC 87.8/86.9/91.8/86.3%)
Average ASR under defenses: baselines 16.23% / 21% / 29.57%, PoiCGAN 67.17%
Clean (no-attack) ACC baseline (Table 1): NEU-CLS 94.4% (LeNet-5), InsPLAD 88.4% (ResNet-18), Kylberg 99.2% (ResNet-34)
T=200 PSG training rounds reported as near-optimal, yielding ~80% ASR with ACC close to 90% in the training-rounds sensitivity experiment (Kylberg)
baselines_compared: TDP - Targeted Data Poisoning (Tolpegin et al., data-poisoning mode of their label-flip/parameter-poisoning framework)
TMP - Targeted Model Poisoning (Bhagoji et al., alternating-minimization update manipulation)
ADA - Attacking-Distance-Aware attack (Sun et al., semi-targeted model poisoning using final-layer gradient information for target selection)
